coredata.dc:title: Leveraging colloid and interface science for long-term marine performance of hybrid coatings: Effects of different nanoparticles on anticorrosion and antifouling properties
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gonzalez-Reyna, M.A.
Espinosa-Medina, M.A.
España-Sanchez, Beatriz Liliana
Perez-Robles, J.F.
Rodriguez, Angel Luis
Esparza, R.
Estevez, M.
coredata.dc:description: This study leveraged the unique colloidal and interfacial properties of synthesized SiO2, ZnO, and carbon (CN) nanoparticles as effective nanofillers to enhance the anti-corrosion and antifouling properties of PMMA coatings. By optimizing nanoparticle dispersion, stability, and functional performance within the polymer matrix, we achieved improved surface roughness, hydrophobicity, mechanical stability, and antimicrobial properties. Hybrid coatings containing 1.5 wt% of these nanoparticles were applied to stainless steel (SS) substrates and evaluated through water contact angle measurements, pull-off adhesion strength tests, antibacterial assays, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The results demonstrate that the synergistic effects of SiO2, ZnO, and CN nanoparticles significantly enhance the coatings' anticorrosion and antifouling characteristics by increasing hydrophobicity, total impedance, and antibacterial activity via reactive oxygen species generated by ZnO and CN. Additionally, over two years, the coating’s long-term protection was assessed by submerging coupons at a 6-meter depth in the Bocana Chica reef barrier, a site with rapid biofouling colonization, making this test a demanding evaluation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrophobic nanoparticles
Nanofillers
Carbon Quantum Dots
PMMA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to CONAHCYT for funding this research through project 845101, \u201CAccelerated Discovery of Antibiofouling Materials.\u201D M.A. Gonzalez-Reyna would also like to thank CONAHCYT for her postdoctoral scholarship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the timeline followed for evaluating the coupons in the Bocana chica reef barrier.
FTIR spectra from the synthesized NPs.

a) Size distribution with their PDI value obtained from DLS of the synthesized NPs using toluene as a common solvent. Micrographs obtained from the synthesized NPS, b) TEM image of the CN showing the presence of carbon spheres and carbon nanodots, c) SEM obtained from the ZnO NPs, and d) SiO2 NPs obtained by the Stöber method.
Graphical representation of the antibacterial activity obtained from ZnO NPs and CN using the microdilution method against S. aureus and P. aeruginosa.

Nyquist plot of SS with and without hybrid coatings at a) t = 0 h and b) t = 24 h of immersion in synthetic seawater. Bode plots of SS with and without hybrid coating at c) t = 0 h and d) t =24 h of immersion in synthetic seawater.
EEC proposed to fit the EIS data of the SS with and without hybrid coatings.
The contact angle of the surface of SS with and without hybrid coatings of PMMA filled with the synthesized NPs individually and in combination.
Antibacterial activity of different formulations of PMMA coating (without NPs, with 1.5 wt% of SiO2, 1.5 wt% Zn, or 1.5 wt% CN). As control was used PVA.

Force-Strain plot illustrating the outcomes of the pull-off strength analysis conducted on the coatings applied to the metal. This curve exhibits the highest force that each coating could withstand before failing and when the failure occurred.
Long-term antifouling performance of coupons with and without coating recovered from the sea at different times showing each coupon's two faces.

a) Before and b) after the biofouling cleaning of a recovered coupon. As seen, a clean surface could be achieved by using water at low pressure, such as through a squeeze washing bottle. Demonstrating the self-cleaning activity of the coating.
Parameters of the EEC (Fig. 6). The values of each EEC element were obtained from the fitting process of the EIS data of each formulation coating.

head.ce:title: Leveraging colloid and interface science for long-term marine performance of hybrid coatings: Effects of different nanoparticles on anticorrosion and antifouling properties

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centro de Investigaciones y de Estudios Avanzados del I.P.N, Libramiento Norponiente 2000, Querétaro, México

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study leveraged the unique colloidal and interfacial properties of synthesized SiO2, ZnO, and carbon (CN) nanoparticles as effective nanofillers to enhance the anti-corrosion and antifouling properties of PMMA coatings. By optimizing nanoparticle dispersion, stability, and functional performance within the polymer matrix, we achieved improved surface roughness, hydrophobicity, mechanical stability, and antimicrobial properties. Hybrid coatings containing 1.5 wt% of these nanoparticles were applied to stainless steel (SS) substrates and evaluated through water contact angle measurements, pull-off adhesion strength tests, antibacterial assays, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The results demonstrate that the synergistic effects of SiO2, ZnO, and CN nanoparticles significantly enhance the coatings' anticorrosion and antifouling characteristics by increasing hydrophobicity, total impedance, and antibacterial activity via reactive oxygen species generated by ZnO and CN. Additionally, over two years, the coating’s long-term protection was assessed by submerging coupons at a 6-meter depth in the Bocana Chica reef barrier, a site with rapid biofouling colonization, making this test a demanding evaluation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: All structures in the marine environment require robust protection against corrosion and biofouling, making the development of effective coatings crucial for the safety and reliability of marine engineering and offshore platforms [1–3]. Corrosion, primarily induced by seawater's aggressive action, leads to the gradual degradation of metallic components, compromising the safety and lifespan of marine assets. Simultaneously, biofouling—the undesirable accumulation of microorganisms, algae, and small marine organisms on submerged surfaces further exacerbates this issue by increasing drag, reducing efficiency, and accelerating corrosion.

Despite the substantial economic impact of corrosion and biofouling on the marine industry, traditional protective measures, such as sacrificial anodes and conventional paints and coatings, have limitations. These solutions often fail to provide long-term protection and may pose environmental risks by releasing harmful substances like heavy metals and biocides into the ocean [1–3]. This underscores the urgent need for more effective and environmentally friendly protective solutions.

The need for more effective and environmentally friendly protective solutions has led to significant interest in developing advanced polymeric coatings [4]. Nanoparticles are crucial components in the design of these hybrid coatings, as their incorporation into the coating matrix can create a synergism of mechanical and chemical characteristics, enhancing the composite coating's overall performance. Researchers have focused on developing nanocomposites based on organic-inorganic materials, such as conventional polymeric and ceramic nanoparticles, to complement their properties [5,6].

Several strategies to avoid corrosion and fouling on structures rely on the use of SiO2 nanoparticles immersed in a matrix coating [6–10], because SiO2 offers several advantages, such as its excellent heat and oxidation resistance, its capacity to provide micro- and nano-scale roughness on the surface (resulting in a superhydrophobic coating), and its enhanced mechanical stability, the hydrophobic surface, a key feature of antifouling coatings, plays a crucial role in resisting the adhesion of fouling organisms and reducing the likelihood of biofilm formation. The presence of SiO2 nanoparticles further reinforces the coating, significantly improving its mechanical durability and corrosion-resistant properties [6–10].

In previous work, our group developed environmentally friendly, corrosion-resistant hybrid coatings for marine environments using PMMA filled with silicon dioxide (SiO₂) nanoparticles [11]. The corrosion resistance of these coatings was related to the size of the SiO₂ nanoparticles added to the polymeric matrix. However, adhesion strength, wettability, and antifouling performance of coatings in marine environments have not been reported.

In addition to SiO₂, other inorganic compounds, such as zinc oxide (ZnO), have also been tested as corrosion inhibitors. For instance, ZnO has been incorporated into various polymeric matrices to improve anticorrosion and antifouling protection efficiency [5,12,13]. Using ZnO offers multiple advantages, enhancing the performance and durability of coatings. For instance, like SiO2 nanoparticles, ZnO creates a micro-nano hierarchical rough structure that produces superhydrophobicity. This rough surface and ZnO's physical and chemical properties improve wettability and corrosion resistance. ZnO also brings additional benefits, including its antimicrobial properties, which prevent the settlement and attachment of biofoulers. At the same time, the release of Zn²⁺ ions offers corrosion inhibition by forming a passive film on the metal surface and damaging microbial cell membranes; for instance, Min Min Aung et al. [14] have proposed using Jatropha-oil-based epoxy acrylate resin filled with ZnO to form nanocomposite coatings. This would improve their anticorrosion protection efficiency (confirmed by the salt spray test in 5 wt% NaCl solution for 792 h) while increasing the contact angle.

There is a growing interest in anticorrosive coatings incorporating carbon nanoparticles like graphene [15], carbon nanotubes [16], carbon nanospheres [17], and carbon dots [18,19] into the polymer matrix, as observed in current literature trends.

These nanostructures, with their unique properties, play a crucial role in achieving anticorrosion and antifouling coatings through different mechanisms [15]. Their dense atomic structure creates a robust barrier that prevents the penetration of corrosive agents and fouling organisms. Their chemical inertness makes them resistant to various corrosive environments, protecting the underlying metal substrate. Their hydrophobic nature reduces the adhesion of fouling organisms, making them a fascinating area of study in materials science and engineering [20].

Moreover, hybrid coatings filled with multiple types of nanoparticles have been commonly studied due to the synergistic effects that can be achieved. The functionalization of nanoparticles further enhances their properties, allowing for the design of coatings with superior anticorrosion and antifouling characteristics. For example, Kamalon Rajitha et al. [21] showed how they enhanced the corrosion resistance properties of Polyurethane (PU) coating by adding zinc oxide/reduced graphene oxide (ZnO/rGO) and zinc oxide/multiwalled carbon nanotube (ZnO/MWCNT). The electrochemical corrosion data revealed the superior anticorrosion efficiency for ZnO/rGO decorated PU coating (99.09 %) compared to that of ZnO/MWCNT dispersed PU coating (95.24 %). On the other hand, the functionalization of NPs is also used to improve their properties, as Ben John et al. [22]. They designed and fabricated a nano-textured superhydrophobic and anti-corrosive polyurethane bilayer coating through the chemical bonding at the interface of hydroxy-terminated polydimethylsiloxane modified cardanol-based polyurethane and silane grafted ZnO nanoparticles. The superhydrophobic surface coating showed a high contact angle of 151° and excellent anti-corrosive properties (Icorr ∼ 10−9 and Ecorr ∼ −0.3 V) as the effect of the silanol and amine functionalities grafted to ZnO nanoparticles that generate a solid inorganic–organic system based crosslinked network with multifunctional properties.

Jiunn-Jer Hwang et al. [23] prepared a PMMA coating with a biomimetic hydrophobic surface of natural Xanthosoma sagittifolium leaf to inhibit bacterial attachment and improve anti-biofilm performance. Using this technique, they increased the contact angle of the coating from 68°±0.8° to 121°±0.6°. The results showed that their coating surface inhibited the growth of bacteria and effectively delayed biofilm formation. The hydrophobicity of the surface also helped to prevent the adhesion of bacteria, thus inhibiting the adhesion of bacteria with good long-term anti-biofilm performance.

Mohammed A. Al-Belushi et al. [24] described the chitosan‑zinc oxide nanorods composite (CHT/ZnO) fabrication for fiberglass panels (support substrate). The composite coatings showed antimicrobial activity under light conditions and high antifouling activity.

Ziyang Zhou et al. [25] presented the distinctive roles of graphene oxide, ZnO quantum dots, and their nanohybrids in waterborne epoxy coatings' anti-corrosion and anti-fouling performance. In this research, the amino-silane functionalized ZnO quantum dots (F-ZnO QDs), graphene oxide (F-GO), and their nanohybrids (F-GO@ZnO QDs) are used as effective nanofillers to improve the anti-corrosion and antifouling properties of waterborne epoxy coatings. The effects of different nanofillers on the surface roughness, water contact angle, pull-off adhesion strength, fracture surface, corrosion resistance, antibacterial and anti-fouling properties of the nanocomposite coatings were studied revealing a multifunctional performance of the epoxy/F-GO@ZnO QDs assigned to the synergy effect between the GO, ZnO QDs, and amino-silane. Nevertheless, the importance of colloidal nanoparticles and their many surface interactions for enhancing polymeric coatings' anticorrosion and antifouling properties must be addressed. Although size and shape are noted and important, surface and interface properties play a significant role in determining the behavior of materials because the proportion of atoms and molecules near a surface or an interface increases dramatically for nanostructured materials.

Building on this body of research, our study proposes a novel strategy to create multifunctional coatings for harsh marine environments. Using PMMA as a polymeric matrix and incorporating carbon nanostructures, ZnO nanoparticles, and SiO₂ nanoparticles as nanofillers, we aim to develop coatings that offer enhanced protection against corrosion and biofouling. The materials were selected by a multidisciplinary group, considering their physical and chemical properties, cost, scalability, environmental impact, and ease of processing. Our results showed that each chosen nanofiller could improve the protection performance of the coating, even after two years of immersion in a coral reef where the conditions to form biofouling are higher than in other places. Together, they contribute to developing more effective, sustainable, and multifunctional protective solutions for marine structures.

Barks of Cinchona pubescens Vahl were obtained from the local market “General Mariano Escobedo” in Santiago de Queretaro, Queretaro, Mexico. Samples were identified according to the guidelines published by the National Herbarium (Instituto de Biología, Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico, Mexico) under registration code IBUNAM: MEXU:93414 [26].

All chemicals and reagents were p.a. grade, purchased from Sigma Aldrich, and used as received without further purification. Methyl methacrylate (MMA) with ≤ 30 ppm of hydroquinone monomethyl ether (MEHQ) as an inhibitor, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), ethanol, ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), sodium chloride (NaCl), zinc acetate dihydrate (99.99 % trace metals basis), and NaOH.
Toluene at 99.98 % was purchased from J. T. Baker and 2,2-Azo-bis-Iso-Butyro-Nitrile (AIBN) at 99 % was purchased from Perkadox.

Hydrothermal technique is a promising method to produce large quantities of sustainable CNs. The hydrothermal reaction is simple, less polluting, and cheap compared to the other methods. It could be carried out with different carbon sources, ranging from simple molecules, such as glucose, to complex molecules, like starch, and various types of biodegradable biomass [27]. For this project, CNs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method using Cinchona pubescens Vahl extract, which produces CQDs with different oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, etc.), as we demonstrated in previous works [28,29]. First, 5 g of the prepared Cinchona pubescens Vahl sample was added to 50 mL of ethanol for a maceration extraction at room temperature for 24 hours. Then, the extract was filtered, transferred into a 100 mL teflon-lined autoclave, and heated at 200 °C for four hours. Afterward, the sample was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. The dried sample was collected and redispersed in toluene to separate the hydrophobic phase of the synthesized CN. The hydrophobic sample was dried again at 60 °C and kept in the dark before further characterization steps. Because the hydrophobic phase is stable enough to be easily redispersed in toluene, and toluene is the solvent used in the preparation of the polymer, this process suggests that the CN will be homogeneously dispersed in the polymeric matrix, keeping a colloidal state and increasing the expected synergistic effect.

The ZnO NPs were synthesized using the co-precipitation method. The coprecipitation method is the most common synthesis for ZnO nanoparticles since it is easier, low-cost, environmentally friendly, capable of enormous-scale production, and employs precursors such as NaOH solutions, which allow for low-temperature processes [30]. The process consists of a precipitation reaction (Eq. 1) of the metal hydroxide and a decomposition reaction activated by heat (Eq. 2).
(1)
Zn
C
H
3
COO
2
+ 2 NaOH → Zn
OH
2
+ 2 Na C
H
3
COO
(2)
Zn
OH
2
→
∆
ZnO +
H
2
O

In this case, an ethanolic solution with 7.22 mmol of NaOH was added dropwise, at a rate of 100 μL/min, to 40 mL of an ethanolic solution with 3.73 mmol of zinc acetate dihydrate under vigorous and constant stirring for two hours at 60 °C, after which solution was allowed to cool down to room temperature [30].

The presence of precipitates was evidence of the formation of Zn(OH)2. The products were subjected to three consecutive washing cycles with ethanol to eliminate unreacted reagents and extract residues. Then, the samples were submitted to a thermal treatment at 60 °C for 24 hours to form ZnO powder, which was stored for subsequent characterization and evaluation [30].

The SiO2 NPs were obtained following the Stöber method described in our previous work [11]. The Stöber method has proven capable of controlling (by systematic variation of reaction parameters) the particle size, distribution, and morphology of SiO2 NPs [11]. Briefly, 10 mol of ethanol, 20 mol of distilled water, and 1 mol of TEOS were mixed in an ultrasonic water bath at room temperature. After 10 min, 1 mol of NH4OH was added to the solution at 300 μL/min and kept under continuous sonication for two hours. Finally, the colloidal solution was placed on a heating plate at 70 °C to remove solvents and collect the SiO2 NPs.

The PMMA was prepared based on the free radical polymerization method described in our previous work [11]. For this methodology, MMA was dissolved in toluene at an 80:20 v/v ratio. The polymerization was carried out after adding AIBN (1:1000 mol ratio against MMA) under reflux at 95 °C with constant magnetic agitation for 90 min and a nitrogen atmosphere to remove oxygen.

The surface of the SS was prepared using an orbital polisher sander with 80-grit sandpaper to remove oxides and guarantee the polymer's good adherence; subsequently, an air compressor deposited hybrid PMMA coatings on the SS surface. The coating was applied in three layers by crossing lines between each layer to reduce the number of imperfections and the final porosity of the sample.

The morphological and structural characterization of the synthesized NPs was analyzed using a Hitachi cold-field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM/STEM) and a Jeol JEM-ARM200F scanning transmission electron microscope (TEM/STEM). The FTIR analysis was recorded from 450 to 4000 cm−1 using a Perkin Elmer Spectrum Two FT-IR spectrometer with a universal ATR accessory with a diamond crystal. The optical contact angle measuring was made with a contact angle meter Dataphysics OCA 50 using synthetic seawater (3.5 wt% NaCl according to ASTM D1141–98 2013).

Quantitative assays of antibacterial activity (AA) of the CN and ZnONPs were performed by microdilution method according to our previous works [28,30] using strains of Gram (+) S. aureus # 6538 and Gram (-) P. aeruginosa #13388 growth in Luria bertani (LB) media. An inoculum of 16 h of growth of each microorganism was adjusted to the final concentration of 1 ×10−6 CFU/mL (working inoculum) by optical density at 600 nm. Each nanostructure was suspended in sterile phosphate buffer (PBS, pH 7.2) with 1 % v/v of Tween 80 as a dispersant. Different concentrations (6400, 3200, 1600, 800, 400, 200, and 100 μg/mL of ZnO and 2000, 1000, 500, 250, 150, and 62.5 μg/mL of CN) were evaluated through the interaction of an equal volume of NPs/ bacteria in a 96-well plate and incubated time of 1 h y 24 h at 37 °C. Duplicates performed three independent experiments. After NPs/bacteria interaction, an aliquot of 50 μL was plated in Mueller Hinton plates (MH) and incubated for 16 h at 37 C. Survival bacterial recovery (AA) was calculated according to our previous works [31,32].

The pull-off strength of the coating to the metal (adhesion) was evaluated using the ASTM Standard D4541 – 02. The general pull-off test was performed by securing a loading fixture (dolly, stud) that was normal to the surface of the coating with an adhesive (J-B Clearweld). After the adhesive is cured (24 h), a testing apparatus (Zwick/Roell model Z005) is attached to the loading fixture and aligned to apply the normal tension to the test surface. The force applied to the loading fixture is then gradually increased (10 mm/min) and monitored until the material is detached.

The EIS data are recorded with a Gill AC potentiostat/galvanostat with a three-electrode electrochemical cell and synthetic seawater. A stainless coupon (5 cm×5 cm) coated with a PMMA formulation was used as the working electrode, a graphite rod as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. The test was performed in the frequency range of 40 KHz to 0.01 Hz with 30 mV amplitude after reaching the steady state open circuit potential (OCP) in synthetic seawater as a corrosive electrolyte.

The anti-bacterial characterization of the hybrid coatings was carried out in agreement with ASTM E2149. Samples were cut into 1 cm2 and placed in a sterile petri dish designed as a humidity chamber. Next, 45 μL of S. aureus inoculum (1 ×105 CFU/mL) was placed at the sample's surface and incubated at 37°C for 3 y 24 h. Subsequently, each sample was washed with 3 mL of PBS for bacterial recovery, and 50 μL was plated in LB agar. Finally, the AA percentage was calculated.

The long-term antifouling performance was evaluated by immersing SS coupons (5 cm×5 cm) coated with a formulation of 0.5 wt% in mass of each NP (total 1.5 %) on the seabed at 6 m deep in the Bocana Chica reef barrier ( Fig. 1). The place was selected because coral reefs are highly biodiverse ecosystems enriched by various biofouling species. Eight coupons coated and eight coupons of SS without coating were immersed to evaluate the antifouling performance against time. The coupons were evaluated for 745 days, which is more than other works reported, and were selected to demonstrate the viability of this kind of coating.

Several factors affect how a material interacts with its surroundings, including surface chemistry, surface area, and surface charge. Therefore, knowing the interface and colloidal stability of the nanoparticles should be pivotal in designing a functional coating with prolonged durability and enhanced protective properties.

The surface chemistry of the NPs plays a leading role in determining the nanoparticle's affinity for biomolecules and, ultimately, its antibacterial activity and anti-biofouling effects. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy is a versatile tool for studying nanomaterial surfaces and how they interact with their media.

The FTIR spectra of SiO2 exhibit ( Fig. 2) the characteristic absorption bands of the silica oxide network. An absorption peak at around 1100 cm–1 is attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of the Si-O-Si bonds. The peak at 820 cm–1 is caused by the symmetric deformation of the Si-O-Si bonds. The vibration of O-Si-O bonds is responsible for the peak at about 470 cm–1. Inorganic materials like ZnO show fewer absorption bands than organic materials, and these bands are usually more intense. Therefore, the FTIR spectra of ZnO samples only displayed an incomplete absorption peak centered around 575 cm–1, which is attributed to the stretching modes of ZnO. The lack of other bands in the FTIR spectra of ZnO implies that the sample only showed one phase, and the other phases would be removed by the thermal treatment or by the consecutive washing cycles.

The absorption peaks of the CNs at 2970–2950/2880–2860 cm–1 correspond to methyl C-H asymmetric/symmetric stretch, while the bands at 1470–1430/1380–1370 cm–1 correspond to methyl C-H asymmetric/symmetric bends. The absorption between 1850–1650 cm–1 is attributed to carbonyl compounds. As seen from the FTIR spectra, none of the samples can form hydrogen bonds, which explains why they cannot be dispersed in water and are easily dispersed in toluene. Besides, the presence of functional groups at the CNs suggests that they could produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), which is the principal antibacterial mechanism of this kind of NP [33].

The morphology and surface-area-to-volume ratio of NPs significantly impact their properties, such as their non-fouling activity and tendency to aggregate [34,35]. Another important characteristic, especially in NPs used as nanofillers, is their suspension stability [36]. To evaluate these properties, it was used DLS, zeta potential, using toluene as a solvent to evaluate the stability ( Fig. 3a). Toluene was chosen because it is a commonly used solvent for PMMA and an organic solvent with high non-polarity. Additionally, the samples were evaluated by electronic microscopy, which gives us insight into the shape and size of the synthesized NPs.

As can be seen from the micrographs obtained from the different NPs, the hydrothermal synthesis produced carbon nanospheres with sizes around 100 and carbon nanodots with sizes around 5 nm (Fig. 3b). The hydrodynamic diameter measurements agreed with the SEM images, where two size distributions were observed for the CN, one related to the carbon dots (mean peak diameter 3.5 nm) and the other to the carbon spheres (centered at 116 nm) with a PDI of 27.1 %. The PDI is high because there are two size distributions in the same sample, even when the dispersion of each peak is small. The ZnO NPs are semispherical, with diameters ranging from 20 nm to 45 nm (Fig. 3c). This result also agreed with the size distribution obtained from DLS, with a mean value of 40.5 nm and close size distribution or PDI of 3.5 %. Finally, the SiO2 NPs present a spherical shape with diameters around 70 and 90 nm (Fig. 3d). Their size distribution has a peak diameter of 98.7 nm with a PDI of 5.06 %. Since all the NPs are semispherical, the shape will not significantly change the properties obtained [35]. Conversely, the sizes will be essential in explaining the non-fouling and anticorrosive effects. Therefore, the carbon dots should produce more considerable changes than the other NPs, followed by ZnO, carbon spheres, and SiO2. Nevertheless, the carbon dots are present in the same sample as the carbon spheres, and their contribution to the non-fouling and anticorrosive effects would be proportional to the amount of each structure. If the proportion of carbon dots is higher than spheres therefore, their effects will be prominent; if their proportion is close to 1:1, their effects caused by the surface-area-to-volume ratio will be like ZnO NPs, but if the proportion of spheres is higher, their effect would be more like the SiO2 NPs.

The mean zeta potential was also obtained; for CN, it was −29.4 mV; for ZnO, it was 39.8 mV; and for SiO2, it was −47.1 mV. From these results, it is possible to conclude that ZnO will be the most stable colloid in toluene. Therefore, ZnO NPs should offer higher hydrophobicity to the PMMA because it is the most nonpolar NPs. The second most stable and nonpolar NPs should be the SiO2 NPs, and the third the CNs.

Marine biofouling occurs when various living and nonliving organisms adhere to the surface of marine structures or vessels, including micro- and microorganisms, plants, and animals [37,38]. Understanding how surface characteristics influence bacterial adhesion is crucial for identifying surfaces that support or resist bacterial adhesion. The current consensus is that a surface's hydrophobicity and zeta potential are important in controlling bacterial adhesion. This knowledge can help in developing surfaces and coatings with antibacterial properties. The coating's hydrophobicity reduces the binding force between its surface and the fouling species adhering to it, making the surface easy to clean. Then, adding hydrophobicity to the surface reduces the adhesion. Besides, to increase the efficiency of the coating as non-biofouling, it is crucial that the coating presents antibacterial activity to slow the formation of the microfouling layer. Thus, it is known that ZnO NPs and NCs show antibacterial activity, which is proportional to the concentration. Then, the CNs and ZnO NPs were evaluated against Gram-positive S. aureus and Gram-negative P. aeruginosa to elucidate their antibacterial activity.

Minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) are defined as the lowest concentration of an antimicrobial that will inhibit the visible growth of a microorganism after overnight incubation. In our case, the MIC was estimated as the lowest concentration that inhibits 80 % of growth. Fig. 4a, demonstrates the antibacterial behavior of ZnO NPs against both bacterial strains. ZnO NPs showed high antibacterial activity against P. aeruginosa, resulting in a 100 μg/mL MIC. In contrast, for S. aureus, their antibacterial activity drops achieved a MIC of 1600 μg/mL. The antibacterial activity exhibited can be attributed to several mechanisms, such as reactive oxygen species (ROS), cell membrane disruption, and release of Zn²⁺ ions [39].

ROS include hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and superoxide anions (O₂•⁻). These ROS can damage the bacterial cell membrane, proteins, lipids, and DNA, leading to cell death. As was seen in zeta potential, the surface of ZnO NPs has a positive charge, which allows them to interact with the negatively charged bacterial cell membrane. This interaction damages the cell surface, causing membrane disruption. ZnO NPs can dissolve and release zinc ions (Zn²⁺) in an aqueous environment. These Zn²⁺ ions can penetrate bacterial cells and interact with intracellular components such as enzymes, proteins, and nucleic acids, inhibiting essential cellular functions and leading to bacterial cell death.

These mechanisms make ZnO NPs effective against a broad range of bacteria, including both Gram-positive and Gram-negative strains. Nevertheless, our NPs present better activity against gram-negative bacteria like P. aeruginosa. The above suggests that the bacterial wall composition is key to obtaining bactericidal ZnO nanostructures. Our group has reported similar results using ZnO NPs synthesized by the coprecipitation method and green methods using Sargassum spp. [40,41].

On the other hand, the CNs exhibit high antibacterial activity against both bacterial strains, with a slightly better performance against S. aureus than P. aeruginosa, achieving MICs of 125 μg/mL and 250 μg/mL, respectively (Fig. 4b). Our group has shown similar results in previous studies, in which we identified the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) as a key antibacterial mechanism [28].

The antibacterial mechanisms of CNs against bacteria are complex and multifaceted, as suggested by the available reports [42,43]. Like ZnO nanoparticles, CNs such as carbon dots and carbon spheres can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and damage the bacterial cell membrane, proteins, lipids, and DNA, leading to bacterial cell death. In addition, they can interact with the bacterial cell membrane due to their surface charge and functional groups. This interaction can disrupt the membrane integrity, causing leakage of intracellular contents. Carbon dots can penetrate bacterial cells and interact with intracellular components such as proteins and nucleic acids. This interaction can inhibit vital cellular processes, such as DNA replication and protein synthesis, ultimately leading to bacterial cell death. An important aspect of carbon dots is that they generally exhibit low toxicity to human cells while being effective against bacteria, as was demonstrated in previous work [29]. Thus, they are promising for applications in medical devices, drug delivery systems, and antibacterial coatings. Therefore, both nanosystems showed favorable results even when CN showed higher antibacterial activity than ZnO NPs. A key shared mechanism between both nanostructures is generating reactive oxygen species (ROS). The size, surface charge, and functional groups of each NPs could influence their performance.

After being characterized, the synthesized NPs were used as nanofillers to enhance the anti-corrosion and antifouling properties of the PMMA coating. The corrosion protection efficiency was assessed using EIS measurements. The electrochemical impedance is an interesting tool for predicting corrosion protection, film porosity, solution absorption into the coatings, and film delamination properties [44]. The evaluation was made by immersing stainless steel (SS) coated with PMMA filled with 1.5 wt% of the NPs individually and in combination. This testing was conducted at the onset and after 24 hours of immersion in a corrosive environment (3.5 wt% NaCl solution).

Fig. 5a shows the Nyquist plots of each coating formulation at the onset of immersion, using the SS without coating as a reference. As can be seen, the hybrid coatings look like ideal coatings, forming curves with big diameter arcs. The inset figure shows a zoom made at high frequencies, where the curves look like almost vertical lines parallel to the “y” axis (tilt angle around 70°). In contrast, the SS presents very low impedance, only clear after zooming the Nyquist plot, showing the characteristic semicircle of this kind of sample. The Bode plots in Fig. 5c showed the impedance module and the phase of each sample at t=0 of immersion in synthetic seawater. The bigger impedance module is for the hybrid coating with SiO2 NPs. Nevertheless, its phase curve showed a resistive behavior in a wide range of frequencies (100 to 104 Hz) and only a capacitive behavior at lower frequencies. Then, considering the impedance module and phase, the coating with better anticorrosive protection is the hybrid coating with all the NPs combined, and the worst is the SS without coating.

After 24 hours, the Nyquist plot (Fig. 5b) only showed changes in the curve sizes, which implies slight impedance variations in all the hybrid coatings except for the SS. This suggests that an oxide layer is formed on its surface, increasing the impedance value significantly and allowing temporal surface passivation. Looking at the Bode plots in Fig. 5d, the SS without coating has the biggest impedance module, followed by the hybrid coating with all the NPs combined, the hybrid coating with ZnO, then with CN, and finally, the one with SiO2. On the other hand, the phase of the SS remains showing a resistive behavior in a wide range of frequencies (100 to 104 Hz) and a capacitive behavior at lower frequencies, while all the hybrid coatings showed a capacitive behavior from 103 Hz to 10−2 Hz. Therefore, the hybrid coatings performed better as anticorrosion protection, acting as insulators that avoid the diffusion of ions through the SS surface, and the best is the hybrid coating with all the NPs combined. Considering that SiO2 NPs are less conductive than the ZnO and CN NPs, the effect that could cause the difference in anticorrosion performance compared with the other NPs could be explained by the chemical inhibitor capacity of these NPs, where ZnO NPs are capable of releasing Zn²⁺ ions that offer corrosion inhibition by forming a passive film on the metal surface while the CNs present functional groups that can coordinate to the metal’s unoccupied 3d orbitals, thus establishing an adsorption film, providing the anticorrosion effect. Therefore, when all the NPs are present in the PMMA matrix, all act together in synergy to improve the anti-corrosion performance.

The data obtained from EIS were modeled by an equivalent electrical circuit (EEC) in Fig. 6 to establish the corrosion parameters. This model is used when blisters or corrosion at the coating/metal interface are often localized. In this circuit, R1 is the solution resistance, R2 is the coating layer resistance, and R3 is the corrosion polarization resistance related to the corrosion in defective and porous areas. CPE1 represents the non-ideal coating capacity, and CPE2 represents the non-ideal capacity of the double layer of electrolyte on the metal surface, the ion permeation process in the holes, and the charge transfer process at the bottom of the holes. The values resultanting from the fitting process of each EIS curve are shown in Table 1. Considering the results obtained for this coating and the significant increase in corrosion resistance, it can be concluded that most of these pores have not reached the surface, and the superhydrophobic properties have improved corrosion resistance.

Different coatings and paints are used to prevent biofouling, and based on their properties; they can be classified into three categories [45]. The first category includes coatings with fouling-resistant properties, which prevent adhesion by providing a physical and free energy barrier to foreign material on a surface [37,38]. These coatings are hydrophilic, hydrogen bond-forming, and electrically neutral. The second category includes coatings with fouling-release properties, which allow weak adhesion of environmental materials to the surface but make it easy to remove them by applying a little shear or mechanical force [37,38]. These coatings are attributed to lower surface energy, such as a hydrophobic surface. The third category includes coatings with fouling-degrading properties, which degrade adsorbed organic material via oxidizing agents and kill bacteria and other microorganisms through bactericidal functionalities [37,38].

Hydrophobicity plays a significant role in enhancing surface anticorrosion and antifouling properties, especially in coatings. We measured its hydrophobic properties using a contact angle to test how well the coating resists fouling ( Fig. 7). We found that the nanocomposite samples had higher water contact angles than the SS without coating and increased with the presence of the nanofillers. This is likely because the nanomaterials in the polymer matrix reduce the hydrophilicity, resulting in higher water contact angles on the nanocomposite samples. The surface's wettability obtained from the hybrid coating with all the NPs combined showed the highest contact angle, reaching a mean value of 92.45°, suggesting a higher fouling-resistant capacity and providing the surface with a higher self-cleaning potential. This hydrophobic effect resulted from the micro-nano hierarchical rough structure produced by the different sizes of the NPs, their interaction, and their non-polarity.

Besides, hydrophobic coatings create a physical barrier resistant to the penetration of corrosive agents such as water, salts, and other chemicals, preventing these agents from reaching the underlying metal and protecting it from corrosion. Therefore, the hydrophobicity induced by the NPs is one of the anticorrosion mechanisms responsible for our EIS results.

Hydrophobic surfaces are less prone to biofilm formation because they reduce the adhesion of fouling organisms like bacteria, algae, and barnacles. It also exhibits a self-cleaning effect, where water droplets roll off the surface, picking up dirt, debris, and microorganisms. Combining both effects, the surface can be kept clean and biofouling-free.

Another essential property of non-fouling coatings is their antibacterial activity. Thus, films composed of PMMA and NPs were tested against S. aureus and P. aeruginosa ( Fig. 8). The formulation with CNs presented higher antibacterial activity, followed by the coating with ZnO NPs as the test to the NPs suggested. Interestingly, the PMMA alone and the PMMA with SiO2 NPs also present a slight antibacterial activity, which may be caused by residues of toluene bonded to the surface of the PMMA and as the effect of the hydrophobic nature of the coating. Another important characteristic is that the antibacterial activity was higher for all the samples against S. aureus, even the system with ZnO NPs, suggesting a synergistic interaction between ZnO and the PMMA.

Considering the importance of good adherence between the coating and the SS surface, a test was conducted to evaluate the adhesion of the PMMA coating to the SS surface with and without nanoparticles (NPs) with each formulation. The results of the test are shown in Fig. 9. All the formulations showed exceptional adhesion to the surface compared to similar coatings found in the literature [25] with an average strength of 30 MPa. Generally, the curves indicate that the adhesion strength between the coating and substrate increases with adding NPs. The best adhesion was observed with the combination of all three NPs, followed by the coatings with SiO2, ZnO, CN, and finally, the PMMA without any NPs. The adhesion strengths were 33.52 MPa, 31.49 MPa, 31.07 MPa, 29.35 MPa, and 24.52 MPa, respectively. The difference in the strain reached by each sample is related to the adhesion failure between the coating and the surface of the SS. The failure occurred partly at the interface between the coating and substrate and partly between the glue and the coating. Therefore, the strain was inversely proportional to the area of interface failure.

Fig. 10 presents a comprehensive analysis of the long-term antifouling performance of coupons with and without coating that were retrieved from the sea at different times (30, 204, 381, and 745 days). The figure includes images of both faces of each coupon, providing a clear comparison of how the coatings have performed over time. Although the samples had a smooth surface before immersion, after 30 days in seawater, there was a noticeable adhesion of microorganisms and soil on both samples, with one face with more adhesion than the other, attributed to one face being more exposed to tidal action. Nevertheless, the surface of the coupon with the polymeric coating was never completely covered, even after 745 days of immersion. In contrast, without any coating, the SS coupon showed a more extensive area and density of fouling species at each evaluated time.

The supplementary section presents a more detailed observation of each coupon. The data presented in the supplementary section can be a valuable resource for researchers, marine biologists, and anyone interested in understanding the effects of antifouling coatings on marine organisms.

The layer of fouling on the coupons looks poorly attached, suggesting that the lack of complete covering could be attributed to the polymeric coating's poor adhesion and self-cleaning capacity. To demonstrate the self-cleaning capacity, all the recovered coupons were cleaned with water at low pressure, using a squeeze washing bottle, as is shown in the supplementary videos. Fig. 11 shows the images before and after the cleaning procedure, showing how the fouling layer could be detached from the surface with low-pressure water. Besides, the integrity of the coating did not seem engaged, and the surface of the SS did not present signs of corrosion, demonstrating a superior anti-corrosion and non-fouling behavior of the hybrid coating even after 2 years immersed in the sea in a reef barrier. Considering the results obtained after characterizing the individual contribution of each NP in the coating, the synergistic mechanism of anticorrosion and antifouling protection of the hybrid coating over time could be a response to the interaction between ZnO's catalytic properties and carbon's conductivity that might enhance ROS generation, leading to stronger antibacterial effects preventing biofilm formation, which is a precursor to more severe fouling. Similarly, the three NPs could work synergistically to enhance the corrosion resistance observed by adding hydrophobicity and mechanical reinforcement. This leads to easier maintenance, enhanced durability, and improved environmental sustainability, making hydrophobicity a key feature in developing effective antifouling coatings.

Hybrid coatings of PMMA loaded with 1.5 wt% nanoparticles of SiO2, ZnO, and carbon (CN) were prepared and applied on stainless steel (SS). The study made indicates that the addition of nanoparticles to the PMMA polymer enhances the anticorrosive properties of the coating and fouling-release capacity by adding hydrophobicity, facilitating the removal of adsorbed foulant by the application of low-pressure water and reducing the adhesion of fouling organisms, preventing, or at least retarding the biofilm formation. On the other hand, CN and ZnO provide fouling-degrading capacity by adding antibacterial activity mainly caused by ROS generation. The coating presents good adhesion that, in combination with the previously said properties, allows a long-term anti-corrosive and anti-fouling protection of SS, as was demonstrated after two years of evaluation of coupons on the seabed at 6 m deep in the Bocana Chica reef barrier.

M. Estevez: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. R. Esparza: Methodology. Angel Luis Rodriguez: Resources, Methodology. J.F. Perez-Robles: Resources, Methodology. Beatriz Liliana España-Sanchez: Resources, Methodology. M.A. Espinosa-Medina: Resources, Methodology. Marlen Alexis Gonzalez-Reyna: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation.

If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to CONAHCYT for funding this research through project 845101, “Accelerated Discovery of Antibiofouling Materials.” M.A. Gonzalez-Reyna would also like to thank CONAHCYT for her postdoctoral scholarship.

ChemElectroChem
